retrieved_docs.title: Candidiasis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A vaginal discharge sample can help to diagnose vaginal candidiasis by examining it under a microscope or by fungal culture in a laboratory. Under the microscope, budding yeast is visible. Oral thrush is mostly a clinical diagnosis but can also be confirmed by looking at the scrapings of the rash under the microscope. For systemic candidiasis, a blood culture is a diagnostic tool. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Sepsis -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Fungal Cultures
retrieved_docs.content: Candida colonization is defined as repeated yeast growth from two separate sites. The CI is a ratio calculated using the number of culture-positive nonbloody body surveillance sites for Candida spp to the number of sites cultured. Samples are typically collected from multiple sites for mycological surveillance; sample sites can include the pharynx, rectum, axillae, urine, blood, and tracheal secretions or gastric contents. Once the CI is calculated, a corrected colonization index (CCI) can be obtained using the ratio of distinct body sites demonstrating heavy growth to those with growth of Candida spp The CCI has been shown to better differentiate between colonized and infected patients. Patients with a CCI >0.4 or CI >0.5 should receive empirical antifungal treatment for Candida . [27] [103]

retrieved_docs.title: Candidemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Unfortunately, blood cultures are limited by their poor sensitivity and slow turnaround time. Results from studies have shown that their sensitivity is only 21% to 71%, although sensitivity can be improved by increasing blood volume and drawing blood cultures more frequently. [31] An essential aspect of having the actual yeast grow in a blood culture is that susceptibility testing can be performed by assessing the minimum inhibitory concentration. Antifungal susceptibility testing can guide targeted antifungal therapy and allow for adjustment of appropriate treatment. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Sepsis -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Fungal Cultures
retrieved_docs.content: The most common causes of fungal infection in critically ill patients include Candida, Aspergillus , and mucormycosis. The primary etiologic agent of fungal infection in the critically ill population is Candida albicans . Serological testing for the components of fungal cell walls is specific for fungal infections but has low sensitivity. RT-PCR has low sensitivity for Candida fungemia but has utility in diagnosing deep-seated candidiasis with negative blood cultures. One screening method for fungal infection in at-risk patients is the Candida colonization index (CI). [103]

retrieved_docs.title: Candida auris -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The identification of Candida auris has presented challenges for laboratories, and it is commonly misidentified using clinical microbiology methods. While the aforementioned phenotypic characteristics of Candida auris colonies in culture may be useful in identifying it, they cannot be used for definitive diagnosis. Many biochemical methods and automated testing methods commonly misidentify C. auris for other Candida species, notably C. haemulonii and other yeast species. [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Candida auris -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnoses of Candida auris include invasive fungal infections caused by other species of Candida and comprise 95% of all invasive fungal infections: [2] Candida albicans Candida glabrata Candida tropicalis Candida paratropicalis Pichia kudriavzevii Other differentials are: Aspergillosis Bacterial sepsis Cryptococcosis Septic shock

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginitis -- Evaluation -- Gold-Standard Tests
retrieved_docs.content: T vaginalis : Nucleic acid amplification test Bacterial vaginosis: Multi-organism polymerase chain reaction (PCR)-based test Vaginal candidiasis: Polymerase chain reaction (PCR)-based test or fungal culture

retrieved_docs.title: Fungal Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- Evaluation -- Candidiasis
retrieved_docs.content: Histopathology of the lesional tissue reveals the increased thickness of the parakeratin layer with embedded candidal hyphae, microabscess in the superficial spinous cell layer, and elongated rete ridges. The underlying connective tissue contains chronic inflammatory infiltrate. [46] Antifungal susceptibility testing is essential in patients who relapse or do not respond to conventional antifungals. [5] CHROMagar culture media can be used to isolate and identify Candida species . Production of germ tubes in the germ tube test indicates the presence of C albicans or C dubliniensis. Immunological methods to detect mannan antigen and anti-mannan antibodies, antimycelial antibodies, or β-D-glucan can be employed in immunocompromised patients when obtaining deep samples is challenging. Matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization-time of flight (MALDI-TOF) mass spectrometry reduces the culture time and is helpful in the identification of candidal species. [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Candidemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Because of the low sensitivity of blood cultures for Candida spp, surrogate markers can also be used to improve diagnostic accuracy. Considering all patient variables, these markers can be considered as supporting evidence when culture results are not yet available in patients at high risk for candidemia. Such markers are Candida mannan antigen, anti-mannan antibodies, and β-d-glucan (BDG). [32] [33] They have the advantage of providing faster results but can have high false-positive rates. The BDG can be used as a negative predictive value. [32] [34] When evaluating a patient suspected of candidemia, 2 sets of blood cultures should be obtained. A non-culture test (eg, BDG), should also be performed to enhance the diagnostic accuracy. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Candidiasis -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Oral candidiasis is a clinical diagnosis. Further evaluation is necessary when a differential diagnosis is required and for cases resistant to antifungal therapy. [8] However, a biopsy is indicated for chronic hyperplastic candidiasis due to its risk of malignant transformation. [8] The commonly implemented methods for identifying Candida are 10% potassium hydroxide stain and culture with sabouraud dextrose agar. [8] Differentiation of specific species is possible with special culture mediums like Chromagar Candida. [9] ELISA and PCR tests are used in cases of invasive candidiasis and to differentiate Candida dubliniensis from Candida albicans . [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Candidiasis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Vaginal and skin infections, although the most common Candida infections are localized. Therefore these can be treated with antifungal drugs to obtain complete recovery and excellent prognosis and outcomes. An untreated Candida infection can affect other organs and may lead to a systemic infection. The long-term prognosis with systemic candidiasis depends on the severity and location of the Candida infection, the general health of the infected person, and the timing of diagnosis and treatment. Almost one-third of the patients with candidemia develop septic shock according to host factors such as age and source of the infection than intrinsic virulence factors of organisms. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Candidemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The Infectious Diseases Society of America has published guidelines and recommendations for the diagnosis and treatment of candidemia in an updated executive summary. [9] When evaluating patients with suspected candidemia, 2 sets of blood cultures should be performed, making sure they are also drawn from an indwelling vascular catheter, if present. Early empiric antifungal therapy should be strongly considered in those who are critically ill with risk factors for invasive candidiasis (eg, the infection is suspected or confirmed, with a positive blood culture for Candida spp). The decision to start therapy should be based on clinical suspicion, risk factors, and surrogate markers of candidemia, such as an elevated BDG. [35] During therapy, it is essential to repeat blood cultures weekly to document the clearance of infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Candidiasis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Cultures and sensitivity tests can be done if antifungal treatment is ineffective. [4] Several methods have been developed to obtain a sample from the oral cavity to identify Candida species. The selection of the method mostly depends on the clinical findings. If visible lesions can be identified, taking a swab with a plain microbial swab or an imprint with a sterile foam pad is recommended. [12] If no specific lesion is identified, but a Candida infection is suspected, collecting a whole saliva sample in a sterile container or the oral rinse technique is advised. [12] If denture stomatitis is suspected, a sample should be obtained from both the internal surface of the denture and palatal mucosa, as sampling from the oral mucosa alone may be negative. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Candidiasis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Vulvovaginitis, or inflammation of the vulva and vagina, most commonly occurs in reproductive-aged women and is caused by several underlying etiologies. Candidiasis is a fungal infection caused by Candida albicans , a polymorphic opportunistic fungus; vulvovaginitis secondary to candidiasis is also known as vaginal candidiasis. Candidal vulvovaginitis is responsible for about one-third of vulvovaginitis occurrences. [1] Typical clinical features include vulvar and vaginal erythema, excoriations, thick white adherent discharge, and swelling. The condition is primarily diagnosed by clinical examination and diagnostic studies, including vaginal wet prep, pH testing, and cultures to exclude other etiologies of vaginal discharge and infection (eg, bacterial vaginosis and gonococcal and chlamydial disease). This activity for healthcare professionals is designed to enhance the learner's competence when managing vaginal candidiasis, equipping them with updated knowledge, skills, and strategies for timely identification, effective interventions, and improved coordination of care, leading to better outcomes for patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Candidiasis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of candidiasis medical conditions and emergencies. Review the steps involved in the evaluation of candidiasis. Outline the management options available for candidiasis. Discuss interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance candidiasis and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Candidiasis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The correct diagnosis of laryngeal candidiasis is difficult for the otolaryngologist, and a high level of suspicion is in order. This condition should also be part of the differential diagnosis in patients with predisposing factors presenting with suspected lesions. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is clinically associated with nonspecific chest pain and with fatigue (التَّعَب). These findings indicate that patients with oral candidiasis may present not only with typical oral lesions but also with systemic or atypical symptoms such as unexplained chest discomfort and generalized tiredness, underscoring the importance of considering this fungal infection in the differential diagnosis when such nonspecific complaints arise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2836
retrieved_docs.content: التَّعَب is a clinical symptom that is commonly associated with تضيق الأبهر and also with داء المبيضات الفموي. In patients with تضيق الأبهر, fatigue often reflects reduced cardiac output and may indicate disease severity, prompting evaluation of valve function and hemodynamic status. Similarly, fatigue can manifest in داء المبيضات الفموي, reflecting systemic effects of the oral Candida infection or related nutritional deficiencies, and may guide clinicians to consider antifungal therapy and supportive care. Recognizing fatigue as a shared clinical feature of these distinct conditions supports more accurate differential diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2768
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain, a symptom that lacks a clearly defined cause, has been reported to be clinically associated with oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي). This relationship suggests that patients who present with unexplained chest discomfort may warrant evaluation for an underlying fungal infection of the oral cavity, particularly when other risk factors for candidiasis are present. Recognizing this association can help clinicians adopt a more comprehensive diagnostic approach and ensure appropriate treatment for both the chest pain and any concurrent oral candidiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2494
retrieved_docs.content: Triazole derivatives, systemic antimycotics constitute a class of antifungal agents that are administered systemically to treat invasive fungal infections. Both itraconazole and voriconazole are members of this triazole derivative class, sharing the characteristic triazole ring structure that confers broad-spectrum activity against a variety of pathogenic fungi. Their classification as systemic antimycotics underscores their use in managing serious fungal diseases where topical therapy is insufficient, highlighting the importance of these agents in comprehensive antifungal treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1655
retrieved_docs.content: The kožní systém (skin) is a primary anatomic site associated with mycoses, meaning that fungal infections commonly involve the skin. Recognizing that mycoses frequently affect the kožní systém is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate antifungal therapy, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1726
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety (القلق) is characterized by fatigue (التَّعَب) and may present with nonspecific chest pain. Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is also clinically associated with both nonspecific chest pain and fatigue. The overlap of fatigue and nonspecific chest pain in anxiety and oral candidiasis highlights the need for careful clinical assessment to differentiate between psychological and infectious etiologies, ensuring appropriate diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 5 -- Community 2933
retrieved_docs.content: Fatigue (التَّعَب) is described as having anxiety (القلق) as a defining characteristic, indicating that patients who experience fatigue often also report significant anxious feelings. Clinically, fatigue is also associated with oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي), suggesting that the presence of persistent tiredness may coincide with or signal an underlying yeast infection of the mouth. Recognizing the link between fatigue, anxiety, and oral candidiasis can aid clinicians in evaluating patients holistically, prompting assessment for both psychological distress and possible infectious contributors when fatigue is reported.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: infections) most commonly involve the skin system, underscoring the clinical relevance of the numerous topical antifungal imidazole and triazole agents described. Collectively, variations of the imidazole and triazole scaffolds generate a spectrum of topical, gynecological, systemic antibacterial and antiprotozoal therapies that guide clinical decision‑making and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 94
retrieved_docs.content: topical anti‑infectives, a broad array of antibacterial medications, gynecological antibiotics and antifungal agents, providing a comprehensive view of their roles in infection control, dermatologic therapy, and related clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2779
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is clinically associated with both anxiety (القلق) and oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي). Anxiety can manifest as vague chest discomfort, making it an important psychosomatic contributor to this symptom. Oral candidiasis, a fungal infection of the mouth, may also be linked to nonspecific chest pain, potentially through systemic effects, medication side‑effects, or underlying immunosuppression that predisposes patients to both conditions. Recognizing that nonspecific chest pain may signal either anxiety or oral thrush—or a combination of both—helps clinicians pursue a more comprehensive evaluation and tailor management to address the underlying cause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1642
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE constitute a broad class of antifungal agents employed in the treatment of skin disorders, and within this class, antifungals for topical use represent the specific subset of medications applied directly to the skin surface to manage superficial fungal infections. Recognizing that topical antifungals are a component of the larger dermatological antifungal category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy based on the site and severity of infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1574
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE and antifungals for systemic use represent two distinct therapeutic categories within the broader class of antifungal agents. Antifungals for systemic use are employed when fungal infections require absorption and distribution throughout the body, whereas dermatological antifungals are applied topically to treat skin‑limited infections. The systemic antifungal category includes specific agents such as terbinafin, which is a member of the systemic antifungal group. Although both categories share the common goal of eradicating fungal pathogens, they differ in route of administration, site of action, and clinical indications. Recognizing the distinction between dermatological and systemic antifungal therapies is essential for selecting the appropriate treatment strategy and optimizing patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 119
retrieved_docs.content: considerations illustrate the hierarchical organization of antifungal, anti‑infective, anticonvulsant and dermatological agents and support clinical decision‑making for both topical and systemic treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2862
retrieved_docs.content: Imidazole and triazole derivatives, topical antifungals represent a pharmacologic class of antifungal agents that includes the specific drugs isoconazole, omoconazole, and tioconazole. All three compounds share the imidazole or triazole chemical scaffold and are formulated for topical use to treat superficial fungal infections. Their classification within the same group highlights their similar mechanisms of action, spectrum of activity, and therapeutic applications in dermatologic antifungal therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 517
retrieved_docs.content: Micafungin is an antifungal medication that is classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “Other antimycotics for systemic use,” indicating its role as a systemic agent for treating fungal infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1642
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE represent a therapeutic class that includes both ANTIFUNGALS FOR TOPICAL USE and ANTIFUNGALS FOR SYSTEMIC USE. By definition, topical antifungal agents are applied directly to the skin, nails, or scalp to treat superficial fungal infections, whereas systemic antifungal agents are administered orally or intravenously to reach deeper or more extensive dermatologic sites. The inclusion of both topical and systemic formulations under the umbrella of ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE reflects the clinical reality that dermatologic fungal conditions can be managed with either local therapy for mild or localized disease or systemic therapy for more severe, widespread, or refractory infections. This hierarchical relationship underscores the importance of selecting the appropriate route of administration based on infection severity, pathogen susceptibility, and patient factors, ensuring comprehensive care for dermatologic fungal disorders.
